paragraphs: I'm Noel, an astronomer based at the University of Warwick. I study some of the most extreme objects in the Universe: neutron stars, black holes, and white dwarfs that are actively stealing material from a companion star. My main interest is in understanding the powerful winds and jets that these systems launch — streams of material flung back into space at millions of kilometres per hour that can shape the fate of the entire binary system.

To catch these fast-changing events, I coordinate observations using many of the world's most powerful telescopes simultaneously — from the Hubble Space Telescope and the James Webb Space Telescope in orbit, to X-ray satellites and giant ground-based observatories in Chile and the Canary Islands. I have been awarded over 190 hours of observing time on these facilities as lead scientist, working with an international network of collaborators across four continents.

My work has led to publications in Nature and other leading journals, including six papers where I am the lead author. I am originally from Spain and completed my PhD at the University of Southampton before moving to Warwick. When I'm not observing, I help develop the instruments and software tools that future astronomers will rely on.

I actively mentor PhD and masters students, sit on telescope time allocation panels for international observatories, and contribute to open-source software projects that make astronomical data analysis more accessible and reproducible.

research.title: Stellar Winds from Dying Stars
body: When a neutron star or black hole siphons gas from a nearby star, it doesn't swallow everything — a significant fraction of that material is flung back out into space in the form of powerful winds. Think of it like a drain: some water swirls down, but some splashes back up. These winds can carry away more mass than actually falls in, profoundly shaping the system's future and even enriching the surrounding interstellar medium.

In 2022 I led the first observing campaign to catch these winds simultaneously in ultraviolet, optical, X-ray, and radio light — like watching the same storm through four completely different lenses at once. The study, published in Nature, revealed that the winds around a neutron star named Swift J1858 were persistent and multi-layered: hot gas, warm gas, and cool gas all flowing outward together, something never seen before in such systems. Read the press coverage on Phys.org →

I am now leading follow-up campaigns on a new set of outbursts — including the brightest black hole flare in nearly a decade — to understand what drives these winds and how they evolve over time. The long-term goal is to build a catalogue of wind measurements that theorists can use to make stellar evolution models more realistic.
research.imageAlt: Artist's impression of a disc wind from a compact binary system
research.imageCaption: Artist's impression of hot gas being flung outward from the accretion disc surrounding a compact object. These powerful outflows, sometimes called disc winds, carry enormous amounts of mass and energy back into space.

research.title: White Dwarf Pulsars: Stellar Lighthouses
body: A white dwarf is the dense, Earth-sized remnant left behind when a Sun-like star dies — a cold, fading ember. But in rare cases, a white dwarf in a binary system can spin fast enough, and carry a strong enough magnetic field, to act almost like a pulsar: sweeping beams of energy across space like a cosmic lighthouse. Only a handful of these 'white dwarf pulsars' are known to exist, and we are only just beginning to understand what makes them tick.

The first was discovered in 2016 — a bizarre system called AR Scorpii, which emits powerful radio and optical pulses roughly every two minutes. I have since identified and characterised a third confirmed sibling of this system (SDSS J230641.47+244055.8), establishing the key observational fingerprints that astronomers can use to hunt for more. This work was published in Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society and highlighted by Nature Astronomy as a paper of special interest.

Using our high-speed camera OPTICAM, I also uncovered the hidden spin signature of a fourth candidate, demonstrating that rapid multi-colour photometry — essentially taking thousands of snapshots per minute in different colours simultaneously — is a powerful tool to expose these exotic objects even when they are disguised as something more ordinary. Together, these discoveries are sketching the outlines of a new class of compact binary star.

research.title: How Binary Stars Evolve Over Billions of Years
body: Binary stars — two stars orbiting each other — make up roughly half of all stars in the sky. When one of them dies and becomes a compact object, the gravitational dance intensifies: matter flows between them, orbits shrink, and the system eventually transforms into something quite different from what it started as. Understanding this evolution is crucial for predicting how many neutron stars, black holes, and double white dwarfs exist in our galaxy — and how many will eventually merge and produce gravitational waves detectable by space-based detectors like LISA in the 2030s.

One of the key missing ingredients in current models is a reliable description of how much mass and energy are actually lost from the system through winds like the ones I study. My observations of Swift J1858.6−0814, combined with the disc wind results, are providing the first empirically grounded constraints on this 'exhaust pipe' of compact binary evolution. I'm also working to connect individual systems to broader population studies, translating one-off detailed snapshots into input that evolutionary codes can actually use.

I recently co-authored a study imaging mysterious shock waves around a white dwarf binary — structures that shouldn't exist according to any known mechanism — which may hint at hidden magnetic processes driving outflows even without significant accretion. Read about the bow shock discovery on Phys.org →
research.imageAlt: Artist's impression of the black hole X-ray binary Swift J1727 during outburst
research.imageCaption: Artist's impression of Swift J1727.8−1613 — one of the brightest black hole outbursts of the last decade — as the black hole tears material from its companion star. Monitoring such events lets us track how accretion discs form, evolve, and eventually fade away.
research.imageCredits: Credit: Instituto de Astrofísica de Canarias (IAC)

research.title: Building Better Telescopes and Tools
body: Great science requires great instruments. I co-led the design and construction of OPTICAM — a triple-channel camera capable of capturing three colours of light simultaneously at extremely high speed, mounted on the 2.1-metre telescope at the Observatorio Astronómico Nacional in Mexico. This is particularly powerful for studying phenomena that flicker and change on timescales of fractions of a second, such as the spin signals from white dwarf pulsars or the rapid variability of accreting black holes.

The £265,000 STFC/UKRI-funded project brought together teams from the UK and Mexico, and OPTICAM has now produced its first published science results — with several more papers in preparation. I serve as Instrument Scientist for the project, overseeing data pipeline development, quality control, and the scientific exploitation of the data. I am also a member of the HiPERCAM team, one of the fastest astronomical cameras in the world.

Alongside hardware, I invest in software: I am developing modernised tools for binary star modelling, building on the legacy of the late Prof. Tom Marsh, whose code has underpinned much of compact-binary science over the past three decades. The goal is to make these powerful but ageing tools accessible to a new generation of astronomers — better documented, modular, and open-source.
